perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of California, USA as applicable to contracts to be performed therein.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by the laws of Outer Space, applicable to contracts performed on Mars.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to 'laws of Outer Space' creates ambiguity. While the contract states it should be enforced in accordance with the laws of Outer Space, such a legal framework doesn't exist, directly contradicting established contract law principles and making enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1636 states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting. Designating 'laws of Outer Space' as the governing law makes the contract's intent ambiguous and difficult to ascertain, directly contradicting the principle of giving effect to the parties' mutual intention under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1636 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1636. A contract must be so interpreted as to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the governing law of the State of California with 'laws of Outer Space, applicable to contracts performed on Mars.' This directly contradicts the principle outlined in California Civil Code Section 1636, which states that a contract must be interpreted to give effect to the mutual intention of the parties as it existed at the time of contracting, so far as the same is ascertainable and lawful. The 'laws of Outer Space' are not an ascertainable or lawful legal framework, making the contract's intent ambiguous and rendering it unenforceable under established legal principles.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall also be “Confidential Information ”; and all Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that all copies of materials and data provided to Buyer (and any information derivative of such information) shall be considered public domain material; and no Confidential Information shall be returned to Broker in the event that Buyer decides not to pursue the Transaction .
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'public domain material' directly contradicts the core intent of a non-disclosure agreement. Public domain information is not protected and can be freely used, while confidential information is subject to restrictions on disclosure and use. This creates a conflict within the contract itself.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) defines and protects trade secrets, which are a subset of confidential information. By stating that confidential information is 'public domain material', the contract effectively nullifies any potential trade secret protection, directly contradicting CUTSA's provisions for safeguarding trade secrets from misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent of protecting confidential information, which is a key aspect of trade secret law, as indicated by the LLM-provided explanation referencing the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'public domain material,' the contract essentially eliminates any protection for trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies California Civil Code Section 3426 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, suggesting that California law protects trade secrets, which is undermined by the changed text. The Perturbation Explanation confirms this contradiction, stating that public domain information is not protected while confidential information is. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not re sulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover, in addition to any damages or compensation received, its costs and expenses incurred in connection with such action or proceeding, including any reasonable attorneys ’ fees, expenses and court costs.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action or proceeding, whether or not resulting in litigation, between Buyer (or either of them) and Seller, or between Buyer (or either of them) and Broker, including any litigation to enforce any of the terms of this agreement, each party shall bear its own costs and expenses, including attorneys’ fees, regardless of the outcome.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the prevailing party's right to recover costs and attorneys' fees, contradicting standard contract enforcement principles and potentially violating California Civil Code regarding contract interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1032
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1032 generally allows a prevailing party in litigation to recover costs. While parties can contractually agree to waive this right, such a waiver must be clear and unequivocal. This modified text, while seemingly clear, contradicts the default rule established by Section 1032 and may be subject to stricter interpretation by the courts, leading to uncertainty regarding cost recovery.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1032. (a) As used in this section, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: (1) “Complaint” includes a cross-complaint. (2) “Defendant” includes a cross-defendant, a person against whom a complaint is filed, or a party who files an answer in intervention. (3) “Plaintiff” includes a cross-complainant or a party who files a complaint in intervention. (4) “Prevailing party” includes the party with a net monetary recovery, a defendant in whose favor a dismissal is entered, a defendant where neither plaintiff nor defendant obtains any relief, and a defendant as against those plaintiffs who do not recover any relief against that defendant. If any party recovers other than monetary relief and in situations other than as specified, the “prevailing party” shall be as determined by the court, and under those circumstances, the court, in its discretion, may allow costs or not and, if allowed, may apportion costs between the parties on the same or adverse sides pursuant to rules adopted under Section 1034. (b) Except as otherwise expres
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the standard practice outlined in California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1032, which generally allows a prevailing party in litigation to recover costs. The changed text states, "each party shall bear its own costs and expenses, including attorneys’ fees, regardless of the outcome," which removes the prevailing party's right to recover costs. Snippet 1 includes: "(b) Except as otherwise expres..." suggesting there are default rules. The modification creates a direct contradiction because it eliminates a right that the law establishes unless explicitly waived. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this eliminates the prevailing party's right. This falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized practices, making the contract potentially unenforceable in regards to cost and expenses for a prevailing party.

perturbation.original_text: Should Buyer present an offer to purchase the Business, Buyer will provide a financial statement and a personal and business history, and Buyer authorizes Broker and Seller to obtain through standard reporting agencies, financial and cred it information about Buyer and/or the companies Buyer represents.
perturbation.changed_text: Should Buyer present an offer to purchase the Business, Buyer will provide a financial statement and a personal and business history, and Buyer authorizes Broker and Seller to obtain through any means necessary, including illegal surveillance, financial and credit information about Buyer and/or the companies Buyer represents.
perturbation.explanation: Authorizing 'illegal surveillance' to obtain financial information directly contradicts laws protecting privacy and prohibiting unlawful data collection, rendering the clause unenforceable and potentially illegal.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution, Article I, Section 1, establishes the inalienable right to privacy. Authorizing 'illegal surveillance' to obtain financial and credit information directly violates this right to privacy, making the contractual clause unenforceable and potentially exposing the Broker and Seller to legal liability for unlawful activities.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Constitution Article I § 1 - Declaration of Rights :: California Constitution :: Justia California Constitution Article I - Declaration of Rights Section 1. Universal Citation: CA Constitution art I § 1 SECTION 1. All people are by nature free and independent and have inalienable rights. Among these are enjoying and defending life and liberty, acquiring, possessing, and protecting property, and pursuing and obtaining safety, happiness, and privacy. (Sec. 1 added Nov. 5, 1974, by Proposition 7. Resolution Chapter 90, 1974.) Disclaimer: This Constitution may not be the most recent version. California may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly authorizes 'illegal surveillance' to obtain financial information. This directly contradicts the right to privacy enshrined in the California Constitution, Article I, Section 1, which states that all people have inalienable rights, including 'pursuing and obtaining safety, happiness, and privacy.' The authorization of illegal surveillance directly infringes upon this right, creating a clear contradiction. The scraped law snippet from Justia emphasizes this right to privacy as a fundamental aspect of individual liberty and protection.
